PETG vs PLA for 3D Printed Tool Inserts: Which Material to Use

Published 2026-03-13 by Chris Winland

You just generated a perfect tool insert with TracetoForge. Now you need to pick a filament. For workshop inserts that live in a climate-controlled shop, PLA is fine. For anything that goes in a truck, van, or job site, you need PETG. Here is why, and when each one makes sense.

The Heat Problem with PLA

PLA has a glass transition temperature around 55-60C (131-140F). That sounds high until you realize the interior of a parked vehicle in summer easily hits 70C (158F). A PLA tool insert left in a Milwaukee Packout on the passenger seat in July will warp. The walls soften, the tool cavities deform, and the Gridfinity base profile loses its snap-fit tolerance.

This is not theoretical. Search any 3D printing forum for "PLA melted in car" and you will find hundreds of posts with photos of ruined prints. If your tool inserts travel with you, PLA is not the answer.

PETG: The Field Service Standard

PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol) has a glass transition temperature around 80C (176F) and a heat deflection temperature around 70C under load. That 15-20 degree margin over PLA means your inserts survive vehicle storage in all but the most extreme desert conditions.

PETG also has better impact resistance than PLA. Drop a Packout case off a truck bed and PLA inserts can crack at sharp corners. PETG flexes and absorbs the impact. For tool organization that takes real-world abuse, this matters.

Side-by-Side Comparison

PropertyPLAPETG
Glass transition temp55-60C~80C
Heat deflection temp~52C~70C
Impact resistanceLow (brittle)High (flexible)
Print difficultyEasyModerate
StringingMinimalMore (needs tuning)
Bed adhesionEasy (PEI or glue stick)Good on PEI, can bond too well
Cost per kg$15-20$18-25
UV resistancePoorFair
Chemical resistancePoorGood

Print Settings for PETG Tool Inserts

PETG prints differently than PLA. Here are the settings that work well for tool inserts on Creality K1C and similar machines:

  • Nozzle temp: 230-245C (start at 235C)
  • Bed temp: 75-85C
  • Layer height: 0.2mm (same as PLA)
  • Print speed: 60-80mm/s (slower than PLA)
  • Retraction: Increase by 1-2mm over PLA settings to reduce stringing
  • Cooling: 50-70% fan (not 100% like PLA)
  • Infill: 15-20% for inserts (walls carry the load, not infill)
  • First layer: Slow (30mm/s) with a slight Z offset increase to avoid bonding too aggressively to PEI

Premium Options: CF-PETG and HT-PLA

If budget allows, carbon fiber reinforced PETG (CF-PETG) adds rigidity without the brittleness of PLA. The carbon fibers reduce warping during printing and make the finished insert feel almost like injection molded plastic. Downside: it is abrasive and requires a hardened steel nozzle.

Polymaker PolyLite HT-PLA is another option. After annealing (baking the finished print at 80C for 30 minutes), its heat resistance jumps to 120C+. The catch is that annealing causes 1-3% dimensional shrinkage, which can throw off tight-tolerance Gridfinity base profiles. For tool cavities it works. For base geometry, test first.

The Bottom Line

Use PLA for inserts that stay in your shop. Use PETG for anything that goes mobile. If you are printing Gridfinity inserts for a Packout case that lives in your work truck, PETG is the only real option. The extra 30 minutes of print tuning saves you from reprinting everything in August.
